coredata.dc:title: Land-atmosphere coupling speeds up flash drought onset
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yumiao
Yuan, Xing
coredata.dc:description: Flash drought is a type of drought with rapid onset and great destructiveness, which poses a serious threat to agriculture, ecosystem, and environment without sufficient early warning. The rapid onset is a unique feature that distinguishes flash drought from conventional dry conditions, but its underlying mechanism remains unclear. With the 42-years reanalysis data, we compared the differences in convective triggering potential and atmospheric humidity indices between flash drought onset and conventional dry conditions over China. We found that the dry land-atmospheric coupling can speed up flash drought onset by suppressing precipitation and increasing evapotranspiration. Results show that the increase of sensible heat transport during flash drought onset can intensify the heating of atmosphere, and enhance the lifting condensation level deficit which efficiently inhibits the convective precipitation. Meanwhile, the atmospheric drying significantly increases the evapotranspiration demand and decreases soil moisture, thus speeds up the drought onset. In this regard, the drier land surface makes the atmosphere drier through land-atmosphere coupling, and the rapid drought onset can be maintained via the positive feedback. Although the contribution of precipitation deficit averaged over China is 92 %, the evapotranspiration excess is also critical for increasing the onset speed especially over South China where the flash drought hotspot exists. With the contribution of evapotranspiration increased by about 26 %, the flash drought onset speed over China almost doubled. This study highlights the importance of dry land-atmospheric coupling for speeding up flash drought onset and provides insights for flash drought diagnosis and prediction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Flash drought
Land-atmosphere coupling
Onset speed
Evapotranspiration

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Flash drought and onset speed definitions
Relative contributions to flash drought onset
The influencing factors of flash drought onset speed

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the definitions of flash drought events and onset speed. The black solid line shows 5 days mean soil moisture (SM) percentile for a grid point (110°E, 27.75°N) during the 2013 flash drought. The red stars and blue diamonds show the onset and recovery stages respectively. The difference between 40th and the lowest SM percentile during the onset stage is SM percentile change, and the duration of the onset stage is onset time. The onset speed is the ratio of SM percentile change to onset time.

Spatial patterns of the (a) flash drought frequency (events/decade) and (b) onset speed (%/pentad) over China. The black squares represent two typical regions, including North China (33°-54°N, 105°-135°E) and South China (18°-33°N, 90°-123°E). The warm-color regions have more frequent flash drought events or faster flash drought onset speed than the cold-color regions. The results are based on ERA5 reanalysis data during the growing seasons (April–September) of 1979–2020. Desert areas and the areas with <3 flash droughts in 42 years are masked out.

Spatial patterns of the composite anomalies of (a) soil moisture decline (△SM = SM(t-1)-SM(t); mm/mm/day) (b) precipitation (P; mm/day) and (c) evapotranspiration (ET; mm/day) during flash drought onset stage, and (d) the spatial pattern of the relative contribution of ET (%) to the water deficit during flash drought onset stages. The anomalies of △SM, P and ET were calculated with reference to the period when the SM percentile is lower than 40 % (for comparing with conventional dry conditions regardless of onset speed). The positive values of (a), (c) and (d) mean that the decline of SM and the increase of ET are more severe during flash drought onset stage than the conventional dry conditions, while the negative values of (b) mean that the P deficit is more severe.

Differences in the probability (%) of convective triggering potential (CTP) and low-level humidity index (HI) combinations between those under the flash drought onset stages and conventional dry conditions (probability during flash drought onset stages minus that during conventional dry conditions) over (a) China, (b) North China, and (c) South China based on the results in 1979–2020. The red area indicates that the probability of such CTP-HI combination during flash drought onset stage is higher than that under conventional dry conditions, while the blue area is the opposite.

The same as Fig. 3, but for the composite anomalies of (a) sensible heat (SH; W/m2), (b) boundary layer height (BLH; m), (c) lifting condensation level (LCL, m), and (d) LCL deficit during flash drought onset stages. The positive values of (a-d) mean that the SH transfer between the land surface and atmosphere, the growth of BLH, LCL or LCL deficit is larger during flash drought onset stage than that under conventional dry conditions.

Relationship between the combination of CTP-HI and the onset speed of flash drought over (a) China, (b) North China, and (c) South China based on the flash drought events in 1979–2020. The combinations of CTP-HI in the black squares are more likely to have strong dry land-atmospheric coupling combined with the dry soil condition, and the warm-color dots mean that the combinations of CTP-HI are usually associated with more rapid onset of flash drought events.

Bar charts of (a) drought onset speed (%/pentad), (b) P anomaly (mm/day), (c) ET anomaly (mm/day), and (d) the relative contribution of ET (%) during the onset stage of flash drought events averaged over China, North China, and South China from 1979 to 2020. Bars with different colors represent different onset speed categories, where lower tercile represents the flash droughts with onset speed slower than 33 % of the speed climatology, and upper tercile represents those with speed faster than 66 % of the climatology.
The same as Fig. 7, but for the regional mean values of (a) CTP (J/kg), (b) HI (K), (c) SH (W/m2), (d) BLH (m), (e) LCL (m), and (f) LCL deficit (m).
Illustration of the land-atmosphere coupling that regulates the onset speed of flash drought.

head.ce:title: Land-atmosphere coupling speeds up flash drought onset

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Hydrometeorological Disaster Mechanism and Warning of Ministry of Water Resources, Collaborative Innovation Center on Forecast and Evaluation of Meteorological Disasters, Nanjing University of Information Science and Technology, Nanjing 210044, Jiangsu, China

Collaborative Innovation Center on Forecast and Evaluation of Meteorological Disasters
Nanjing University of Information Science and Technology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Hydrology and Water Resources
Nanjing University of Information Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Hydrology and Water Resources, Nanjing University of Information Science and Technology, Nanjing 210044, Jiangsu, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Hydrology and Water Resources
Nanjing University of Information Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Flash drought is a type of drought with rapid onset and great destructiveness, which poses a serious threat to agriculture, ecosystem, and environment without sufficient early warning. The rapid onset is a unique feature that distinguishes flash drought from conventional dry conditions, but its underlying mechanism remains unclear. With the 42-years reanalysis data, we compared the differences in convective triggering potential and atmospheric humidity indices between flash drought onset and conventional dry conditions over China. We found that the dry land-atmospheric coupling can speed up flash drought onset by suppressing precipitation and increasing evapotranspiration. Results show that the increase of sensible heat transport during flash drought onset can intensify the heating of atmosphere, and enhance the lifting condensation level deficit which efficiently inhibits the convective precipitation. Meanwhile, the atmospheric drying significantly increases the evapotranspiration demand and decreases soil moisture, thus speeds up the drought onset. In this regard, the drier land surface makes the atmosphere drier through land-atmosphere coupling, and the rapid drought onset can be maintained via the positive feedback. Although the contribution of precipitation deficit averaged over China is 92 %, the evapotranspiration excess is also critical for increasing the onset speed especially over South China where the flash drought hotspot exists. With the contribution of evapotranspiration increased by about 26 %, the flash drought onset speed over China almost doubled. This study highlights the importance of dry land-atmospheric coupling for speeding up flash drought onset and provides insights for flash drought diagnosis and prediction.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: “Flash drought” was proposed by Svoboda et al. (2002) to describe drought events with rapid onset speed, which has been found to occur frequently around the world in recent years, resulted in significant impact on agriculture and environment (Gerken et al., 2018; Li et al., 2020; Nguyen et al., 2019; Otkin et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2016; Yuan et al., 2015, 2019). As the most important characteristic of flash drought, the rapid onset may cause more unexpected losses than conventional drought with slow evolvement (Otkin et al., 2018; Zhang and Yuan, 2020), and its impact on terrestrial carbon dynamics and ecosystem productivity should not be overlooked either (Jin et al., 2019; Yao et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2020). What's more, the rapid onset also poses a great challenge to the current drought monitoring and early warning systems (PaiMazumder and Done, 2016; Pendergrass et al., 2020). With the acceleration of flash drought onset due to anthropogenic climate change (Wang and Yuan, 2021), there is an urgent need to understand the physical mechanism of the rapid onset process.

Severe precipitation deficit is found to be an important factor to arouse drought (Koster et al., 2019; Leng and Hall, 2019; Noguera et al., 2021). However, the precipitation deficit alone cannot fully explain why flash drought develops more quickly than conventional drought, and the role of evapotranspiration needs further investigation. During drought conditions, the variation of evapotranspiration is complex. On one hand, low soil moisture (SM) limits soil evaporation and induces stomatal closure to prevent transpiration, leading to reductions in evapotranspiration. On the other hand, the drought-associated meteorological conditions such as high temperature elevate atmospheric evaporative demand, which is efficient for increasing evapotranspiration and exacerbating drought conditions (Diao et al., 2021). Mo and Lettenmaier (2015, 2016) divided the flash droughts into heatwave and precipitation deficit droughts and believed that the strong evapotranspiration under high-temperature is the main driver for the onset of heatwave flash droughts. Nevertheless, a few recent case studies about extreme flash drought events found that the contribution of evapotranspiration was not always positive (Basara et al., 2019; He et al., 2019; Nguyen et al., 2019). Koster et al. (2019) divided the evapotranspiration from the perspective of water and energy control, and found that the contribution of excess evapotranspiration during the first five days is larger than that in the whole period of flash drought, which again aroused the speculation that the evapotranspiration may be important for the onset of flash drought. Therefore, it is necessary to associate the precipitation deficit and evapotranspiration excess with the flash drought onset speed, and further explore the underlying mechanism.

Land-atmosphere coupling can influence the energy distribution and water exchange by regulating evapotranspiration and precipitation (Miralles et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2019), and further affect extreme hydro-meteorological extremes (Miralles et al., 2014; Seneviratne et al., 2006; Teuling et al., 2010). The synergistic drying between SM and the atmosphere under the positive feedback between them has been proved to be an important mechanism of drought development (Qing et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2019), and the dry coupling between the land surface and atmosphere has also been used to diagnose and predict the propagation of drought events (Roundy et al., 2014; Roundy and Wood, 2015; Wakefield et al., 2019), during which the drought condition can be maintained and strengthened (Teuling et al., 2010, 2013; Zeng et al., 2019). As for flash drought, the important influence of land-atmosphere coupling has also attracted attention in recent years. Gerken et al. (2018) found that the dryness of atmosphere effectively decreased the likelihood of convective precipitation before the onset of the 2017 flash drought over the U.S. Northern Great Plains. Basara et al. (2019) found that the spatial propagation of the 2012 flash drought over the U.S. is closely related to the suppression of local atmospheric convection and the increase of evaporation stress. However, most previous studies only focused on the dry coupling characteristics for a few flash drought cases and ignored the underlying mechanism of the onset process, whether the land-atmosphere coupling can significantly affect the onset speed of flash drought is still unknown, which needs comprehensive investigations based on a large number of flash droughts.

Here, we focus on the changing characteristics of precipitation and evapotranspiration during the onset process of flash droughts over China for the past 42 years (1979–2020), and investigate the physical mechanism of rapid onset from the perspective of land-atmosphere coupling. The purpose of this study is to answer the following questions: 1) What are the characteristics of hydro-meteorology and land-atmosphere coupling that promote the rapid onset of flash drought events? 2) What are the contributions of precipitation deficit and evapotranspiration excess to the rapid onset speed of flash drought? 3) How does the land-atmosphere coupling regulate the onset process of flash drought and then affect the flash drought onset speed?

The fifth-generation reanalysis data (ERA5; Hersbach et al., 2020) released by the European Center for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF) were used in this study. ERA5 combined model data with observations from across the world to form a globally complete dataset, and the applicability of this dataset over China was verified for different variables from different perspectives (Meng et al., 2018; Song et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2021; Zeng et al., 2021; Zhu and Yuan, 2019). The hourly root-zone SM (top 1 m, interpolated from 0-7 cm, 7-28 cm, 28-100 cm SM), precipitation, and evapotranspiration were converted to pentad-mean and used for identifying the flash drought events and investigating the associated hydro-meteorological characteristics. The land-atmospheric coupling indexes were calculated at hourly timescale and then averaged to pentad-scale by using 2-m temperature, 2-m dew point temperature, sensible heat (SH), boundary layer height (BLH), and surface air pressure, as well as the atmospheric specific humidity and temperature from 50 to 1000 hPa (29 layers in total). The study period is from 1979 through 2020 with a focus on the growing season (April through September) over China. The spatial scale of ERA5 data used in this study is 0.25degree (25 km).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flash drought and onset speed definitions
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the rapid decline of SM percentile and duration of drought events, Yuan et al. (2019) proposed a method for explicitly characterizing flash drought events, which could fully describe the onset and recovery processes, and was widely used in flash drought research (Mukherjee and Mishra, 2022; Qing et al., 2022; Zhang and Yuan, 2020; Zhu and Wang, 2021). Here, the definition of flash drought was derived according to Yuan et al. (2019). To characterize the rapid evolution of flash drought at sub-seasonal time scale but remove the noise of weather events, the hourly SM data was converted to pentad-mean SM percentile firstly, and the following three criteria were used:
1)
The pentad-mean SM should decrease from above 40th to 20th percentile, with an average decline rate (from 40 % to current) ≥5 %/pentad to highlight the “flash” characteristic.
2)
The flash drought is considered to be terminated if the SM rises above 20th percentile again, and the drought must last for at least 4 pentads (20 days) to eliminate short dry spells.
3)
During a flash drought event, the period when the SM percentile decreases and the average decline rate ≥5 %/pentad is considered as the onset stage (red stars in Fig. 1 ), and the remaining period is the recovery stage (blue diamonds in Fig. 1).

Specifically, the average decline rate of pentad-mean SM percentile during the onset stage is regarded as the onset speed. As shown in Fig. 1, the difference between 40th and the lowest percentile during the onset stage is regarded as the SM percentile change, and the corresponding time is onset time. The onset speed for flash drought is then defined as the ratio of SM percentile change to onset time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Relative contributions to flash drought onset
ce:section.ce:para:
In this study, the period when the pentad-mean SM is lower than 40th percentile is regarded as the conventional dry conditions, and the soil moisture decline is calculated based on the differences of soil moisture between the previous pentad and the current pentad (△SM = SM(t-1)-SM(t)). Firstly, the multi-year mean values of △SM, precipitation and evapotranspiration during the conventional dry conditions from 1979 to 2020 were calculated for each target pentad. Then, taking these multi-year pentad mean values as climatologies, the anomalies of pentad-mean △SM, precipitation and evapotranspiration during onset stage of each flash drought event were calculated by comparing with their corresponding climatologies, and were averaged to carry out composite analysis to explore the reasons for promoting the rapid onset of flash drought. According to the equation of water balance, precipitation and evapotranspiration are the main factors affecting △SM, and the relative contribution of evapotranspiration to the water deficit (ET-P) during flash drought onset can be quantified as:
(1)
ET
contribution =
ET
ET − P
,
where ET and P (mm/day) express the anomalies of evapotranspiration and precipitation during flash drought onset, respectively. With this method, the area where ET - P < 0 (26 points, 0.24 %), ET - P > 0 & ET contribution > 1 (24 points; 0.22 %), or ET - P > 0 & ET contribution < −1 (11 points; 0.1 %) were excluded to ensure a reasonable estimation of ET contribution.

The atmospheric boundary layer (ABL) is the bottom portion of the atmosphere, which is in direct contact with the land surface and is crucial to land-atmosphere coupling (Wei et al., 2021a). Convective triggering potential (CTP) can express the thermal stability of the ABL, and it is the vertical integral of the area between the ambient temperature profile and the moist adiabat from 100 hPa to 300 hPa above the surface (Findell and Eltahir, 2003):
(2)
CTP = g
∫
Z
PSurf − 300
Z
PSurf − 100
T parcel
−
T env
T env
dz ,
where g (m/s2) is the gravitational acceleration, Z
PSurf-100 and Z PSurf-300 (m) are the heights of 100 hPa and 300 hPa above the surface, T
parcel and T
env (K) are the temperatures of a saturated air parcel and the environment respectively, and dz (m) is the thickness of the atmospheric layer. When the temperature profile is closer to dry adiabat, the CTP is larger and the ABL is more unstable.

Low-level humidity index (HI) was proposed by Litynska et al. (1976) to describe the water vapor condition in the ABL, which is defined as the sum of the differences between temperature and dewpoint temperature (dewpoint depressions) at 50 and 150 hPa above the ground level, and a higher HI expresses a drier ABL:
(3)
HI =
T
PSurf − 50
−
T
d , PSurf − 50
−
T
PSurf − 150
−
T
d , PSurf − 150
,
where T
PSurf-50 and T
PSurf-150 (K) are the temperature at 50 and 150 hPa above the ground level, respectively. T
d,PSurf-50 and T
d,PSurf-150 (K) are the dewpoint temperature at 50 and 150 hPa above the ground level, respectively. Combined with the SM conditions, the land-atmospheric coupling state can be distinguished with the unique combination of CTP and HI (Findell and Eltahir, 2003; Wakefield et al., 2019). The CTP and HI were calculated at 0400 local time and then averaged to pentad-mean in this study.

Furthermore, the lifting condensation level (LCL; Lawrence, 2005) and LCL deficit were calculated to reflect the dynamic and water vapor conditions of the ABL:
(4)
LCL =
T −
T d
Г d
−
Г dew
,
(5)
LCL
deficit = LCL − BLH ,
where Г
d (about 9.8 K/km) and Г
dew (about 1.8 K/km) are the dry adiabatic and dew point vertical decline rates, respectively. T and T
d (K) are the 2-m temperature and 2-m dew point temperature, respectively. BLH is the height of the ABL, which can be obtained from the ERA5 dataset, and only when the dynamic development of the ABL exceeds the water vapor limit (LCL deficit <0) can arouse convective precipitation.

With the above indexes, we try to understand the characteristics of the ABL and the land-atmospheric coupling states in the process of flash drought onset. In addition, whether the coupling can affect the onset speed of flash drought by regulating evapotranspiration and/or precipitation is also what we concerned.

Fig. 2 depicts the spatial distributions of the frequency and average onset speed of flash drought over the continental China. We found that South China (18°-33°N, 90°-123°E) is a hot spot for flash drought, where the frequency and onset speed are significantly higher than those in North China (33°-54°N, 105°-135°E). The average frequency of flash drought events over South China is about 3.9 events/decade, and the onset speed mostly concentrates in 10–13 %/pentad. While over North China, the average frequency is about 2.8 events/decade, and the onset speed mainly concentrates in 9–11 %/pentad. Such spatial pattern is consistent with the existing studies (Liu et al., 2020; Yuan et al., 2019), and is closely related to the spatial distribution of SM over China. Under the condition of high temperature and strong radiation, the decline of SM over South China is quick, which is favorable for the rapid onset of flash drought.

With the anomalies of △SM, precipitation and evapotranspiration, we carried out the composite analysis to investigate the hydro-meteorological characteristics during the onset of flash drought as compared with those during conventional dry conditions regardless of onset speed (Fig. 3a-c). Furthermore, the relative contribution of evapotranspiration (Fig. 3d) was calculated according to Eq. (1), and the spatial distributions were shown. At the onset stage of flash drought, the anomalies of △SM, precipitation and evapotranspiration are obvious in almost the whole region of China, which suggests that the severe precipitation deficit and strong evapotranspiration excess are important reasons to promote the rapid onset of flash drought as compared with the conventional dry conditions. Especially over South China, more significant precipitation deficit and evapotranspiration excess correspond to more frequent and rapid onset of flash drought. From the perspective of water balance, the precipitation deficit is much higher than evapotranspiration excess during the flash drought onset (Fig. 3b-c), and the relative contribution of evapotranspiration (7.8 %) is much smaller than that of precipitation (92.2 %) over China. Therefore, the accumulation of precipitation deficit is the main reason for the water deficit during the flash drought onset (Koster et al., 2019; Noguera et al., 2021).

Furthermore, we compared the probabilities of different CTP-HI combinations during the flash drought onset stages and the conventional dry conditions from the perspective of land-atmosphere coupling and showed their differences (probability during flash drought onset stages minus probability under conventional dry conditions) in Fig. 4a-c. For the results averaged over China, the probability for the CTP between 0 and 200 J/kg and HI between 8 and 20 K increases significantly during onset periods. The combination of CTP and HI in this regime is more likely to trigger precipitation over wet rather than over dry soils through land-atmosphere coupling (Findell and Eltahir, 2003; Ferguson and Wood, 2011). The ABL is relatively unstable while abnormally drier than normal, coupled with the dry soil during flash drought, the inhibition of precipitation by water limitation and the increase of evapotranspiration demand can dry the atmosphere and soil simultaneously, and the positive feedback between the land surface and atmosphere is manifested as the dry land-atmospheric coupling. In addition, the composite analysis of SH, BLH, LCL, and LCL deficit (Fig. 5a-d) were also carried out to further understand the process of energy and water vapor transfer during dry land-atmospheric coupling. It is found that the flash drought onset is always companied with a stronger SH transfer between the land surface and atmosphere compared with the conventional dry conditions, which promotes the growth of LCL and BLH under the influence of warm-dry air entrainment, and suggesting the drying and heating of the atmosphere (Wei et al., 2021b). The increase of LCL deficit means that the growth of BLH can't break through the limitation of water vapor, so the convective precipitation will be restrained (Fig. 3b). Meanwhile, the drying of near-surface atmosphere further increases evapotranspiration (Fig. 3c), which is of great significance for promoting the rapid onset of flash drought (Figs. 2a-b, 3a).

Although South China is a humid region controlled by monsoon climate, it can present significant land-atmosphere coupling when the SM is dry enough (Zeng and Yuan, 2018). During flash drought onset, there is more significant enhancement (compared with the conventional dry conditions) of dry land-atmospheric coupling over South China (Fig. 4c) than that over North China (Fig. 4b), and the increase of energy transfer from the land surface to the atmosphere is more intense over South China (Fig. 5a). This phenomenon is of great significance for restraining precipitation and promoting evapotranspiration (Fig. 3b-c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The influencing factors of flash drought onset speed
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, the characteristics of hydro-meteorological conditions and land-atmospheric coupling indices were associated with the onset speed of flash drought for further investigation. The relationship between the combinations of CTP-HI and the onset speed of flash droughts was shown in Fig. 6 . Under the conditions of “0 J/kg <CTP<200 J/kg & 5 K<HI<25 K” (black squares in Fig. 6), the ABL is relatively unstable while abnormally drier than normal. Combined with the dry soil condition, it can effectively suppress convective precipitation and is more likely to have a strong dry land-atmospheric coupling (Findell and Eltahir, 2003; Ferguson and Wood, 2011). Correspondingly, the flash drought onset speed is also much higher, which means that the dry land-atmospheric coupling plays an important role in accelerating the onset speed of flash drought. Furthermore, for each grid point over the study area, we classified the flash drought events into three groups based on their onset speeds (speeds for upper tercile, normal speeds, and speeds for lower tercile), and showed the spatiotemporal mean values of flash drought onset speed, precipitation anomaly, evapotranspiration anomaly, and evapotranspiration contribution (Fig. 7a-d) for the flash drought events with the speeds for upper and lower terciles from 1979 to 2020. With the exacerbation of precipitation deficit and evapotranspiration excess, the soil water depletion is sped up, which results in a more rapid onset speed of flash drought. At the same time, the relative contribution of evapotranspiration increases accordingly, which partly explains the increase of the flash drought onset speed. By comparing the results for the upper and lower terciles, with the evapotranspiration excess increased by about 80 % and its contribution increased by about 26 %, the onset speed over China almost doubled, which highlights the important role of evapotranspiration to regulating the drought onset speed.

Furthermore, we tried to explore the above phenomenon from the perspective of land-atmosphere coupling. The anomalies of CTP, HI, SH, BLH, LCL, and LCL deficit were calculated, and the comparisons of mean values between the upper and lower terciles were shown (Fig. 8a-f). Compared with the lower tercile of flash drought over China, the SH transfer of the upper tercile increased by about 43.7 % ((SHupper tercile-SHlower tercile)/SHlower tercile*100 %), which aroused the growth of BLH and the increase of LCL by 61.3 % and 78.2 %, respectively. With the LCL deficit almost doubled, the suppression of convective precipitation was also more significant, and the precipitation deficit increased by about 40.7 % (Fig. 7b). Meanwhile, the dry coupling between the land surface and atmosphere is strengthened (Fig. 8a-b). Over South China where the flash drought events are more frequent with more rapid onset than over North China, there is a stronger increase from the lower tercile to the upper tercile for the land-atmospheric coupling indexes, and the precipitation deficit, evapotranspiration excess and the relative contribution of evapotranspiration (Fig. 7b-d) are also more significantly increased than that over North China. In short, the rapid onset speed of flash drought is associated with the strong dry land-atmospheric coupling and severe water deficit.

Fig. 9 illustrates the physical mechanism of land-atmosphere coupling in affecting flash drought onset speed through regulating precipitation and evapotranspiration. During flash drought onset stage, the SH transfer between the land surface and atmosphere increases obviously and promotes the growth of LCL due to the warm-dry air entrainment, which means the drying and heating of ABL. On one hand, the increase of LCL deficit inhibits the convective precipitation, which is the main cause of the water deficit during flash drought onset. On the other hand, a drier atmosphere means an increase in the demand for atmospheric evapotranspiration, which can accelerate the consumption of SM by increasing evapotranspiration significantly, and further speed up the flash drought onset. In this regard, the drier land surface makes the atmosphere drier through dry land-atmospheric coupling, so that the positive feedback can be maintained. From the perspective of land-atmosphere coupling, the role of strong evapotranspiration during flash drought onset is mainly reflected in regulating the onset speed, which is essential for distinguishing flash drought from conventional drought.

In this study, we investigated the hydro-meteorological and land-atmosphere coupling characteristics during flash drought onset based on 42-years reanalysis data, and further analyzed the physical mechanism of rapid onset speed. The main conclusions are as follows:
1)
During flash drought onset stage, most regions over China experienced severer precipitation deficit, stronger evapotranspiration, and more significant dry land-atmospheric coupling than those during conventional dry conditions, which promotes the rapid decline of SM and is conducive to the rapid onset of flash drought.
2)
Through comparing the flash drought onset stage with conventional dry conditions, we found that the contribution of precipitation deficit is 92 % over China, which is dominant for the water deficit during flash drought onset. Moreover, the evapotranspiration excess can further regulate the onset speed through land-atmosphere coupling. With the evapotranspiration excess increased by about 80 % and its contribution increased by about 26 %, the onset speed over China almost doubled from lower to upper terciles of speed climatology, which is important for distinguishing flash drought from conventional drought.
3)
Under the dry land-atmospheric coupling, the strong SH transfer between the land surface and atmosphere promotes the drying and heating of the ABL, which further accelerates the onset speed of flash drought by inhibiting the convective precipitation and increasing evapotranspiration. Compared with North China, the enhancement of dry land-atmospheric coupling over South China is stronger during flash drought onset, which leads to a faster onset speed of flash drought.

Our study not only quantified the relative contributions of precipitation and evapotranspiration but also revealed the physical mechanism of regulating the onset speed of flash drought from the perspective of land-atmosphere coupling. It can provide a theoretical basis for the diagnosis and prediction of flash drought events, which is of great significance for the mitigation of drought disasters.

During flash drought onset, though the contribution of evapotranspiration excess is much smaller than precipitation deficit in terms of water budget, the evapotranspiration can regulate the onset speed of flash drought, which distinguishes flash drought from conventional drought with slow development. An in-depth understanding of the evapotranspiration constraints in different stages of flash drought is also of great significance to understand the evolution process and spatial patterns of flash drought. What's more, accurate division of the land-atmospheric coupling states with the CTP-HI framework over different regimes is difficult, which may cause the uncertainty of the results. Using different thresholds over different regimes is necessary to reasonably identify the land-atmospheric coupling states (Ferguson and Wood, 2011; Roundy et al., 2013). Besides the land-atmosphere coupling, the influences of atmospheric and oceanic anomalies on flash drought onset should not be overlooked either. The drought events over North China is closely related to the weakened East Asian summer monsoon and the southward movement of western Pacific subtropical high (Zhang et al., 2003), while the positive phase of the Atlantic Multi-decadal Oscillation may cause drought events over South China (Ding et al., 2018). These multi-scale processes may also affect the sub-seasonal climate variability, and their role in triggering flash drought needs further investigation. In addition to the local land-atmosphere coupling, the important influence of tele-connected land-atmosphere and ocean-atmosphere coupling on extreme hydro-meteorological events has been confirmed in many studies (Alizadeh et al., 2020; Liu and Zhou, 2021; Miralles et al., 2019; Schumacher et al., 2019; Zeng et al., 2019), and it may be a direction worthy of consideration for further investigating the physical mechanism of flash drought onset. Finally, in the context of climate change, how do the characteristics of land-atmosphere coupling change in the hotspots of flash drought and how they will further affect the onset of flash drought are also issues of great concerns for further studies.
Yumiao Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft. Xing Yuan: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (41875105), National Key Research and Development Program of China (2018YFA0606002), and Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province for Distinguished Young Scholars (BK20211540).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, M., Yuan, X., Otkin, J., 2020. Remote sensing of the impact of flash drought events on terrestrial carbon dynamics over China. Carbon. Manag. 15(1), 20, doi:10.1186/s13021-020-00156-1.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhang et al., 2003

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The decadal and interannual variations of drought in northern China and their association with the circulations

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, Q., Wei, J, Tao, S., 2003. The decadal and interannual variations of drought in northern China and their association with the circulations. Climatic Environ. Res. 8, 307–318. doi:10.3969/j.issn.1006-9585.2003.03.005.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhou et al., 2019

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Land-atmosphere feedbacks exacerbate concurrent soil drought and atmospheric aridity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhou, S. Williams, A. P., Berg, A. M., Cook. B. I., Zhang, Y., Hagemann, S., Lorenz, R., Seneviratne, S. I., Gentine, P., 2019. Land-atmosphere feedbacks exacerbate concurrent soil drought and atmospheric aridity. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U.S.A. 116(38), 18848-18853. doi:10.1073/pnas.1904955116.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhu and Yuan, 2019

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Applicability of ERA reanalysis data of land surface temperature in Zhejiang Province

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhu, J., Yuan, H., 2019. Applicability of ERA Reanalysis Data of Land Surface Temperature in Zhejiang Province. Meteor. Sci. Technol. 47(2), 289-298. doi:10.19517/j.1671-6345.20180171. (in Chinese)

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhu and Wang, 2021

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The diagnosis about spatiotemporal characteristics and driving factors of flash drought and its prediction over typical humid and semiarid basins in China

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhu, Q., Wang, Y., 2021. The Diagnosis about Spatiotemporal Characteristics and Driving Factors of Flash Drought and Its Prediction over Typical Humid and Semiarid Basins in China. J. Hydrometeorol. 22(10), 2783-2798. doi:10.1175/JHM-D-21-0062.1.
